Handlers Effects & replay

rewindjs.com
Overview

Handlers are pure functions.

rewind.js is a serverless JavaScript platform with one defining property: a handler is a pure function of its inputs, every external effect is recorded, and so any production request can be replayed later — exactly, as many times as it takes to understand it.

pure functions taped effects replayable state

The mental model

If you know The Elm Architecture, you already know the shape. A handler module is one update : (Msg, Model) → (Model', Cmd) function, with each case-arm hoisted to its own export:

  • Model is the tenant's kv — durable, replicated, read-your-writes within an activation.
  • Msg is the runtime's fixed set of activation kinds. The default export handles inbound HTTP — the 80% case; everything else (timers, callbacks, subscriptions) is an opt-in named export.
  • Cmd is the effects you call in the body — kv.* builds the model; stream.*, on.*, and the connectionless verbs queue and fire after the write commits.

Inside an activation your code is ordinary imperative JavaScript. What makes it pure is the boundary: every non-deterministic read (the clock, randomness, an outbound fetch's response) is captured, and an effect may return data to your code only as a recorded message.

Hello, handler

The minimal handler is a function that returns a response body. Return a string (or any value — it's JSON-encoded); the status defaults to 200.

export default function () {
  const n = parseInt(kv.get("hits") ?? "0", 10) + 1;
  kv.set("hits", String(n));     // durable, replicated
  return { message: "hello from rewind.js", hits: n };
}

No fetch, no setTimeout, no ambient I/O in the body — those are effects, reached through the verb surface (see Handlers). That restriction is the whole point: it's what makes the next part possible.

Why pure? Because replay.

Because the inputs are durable and the outputs are derivable from them, the platform can take any production request and run it again — same inputs, same recorded effects, same result, byte for byte. The bug report is the reproduction. Debugging stops being archaeology and becomes time travel.

Determinism isn't an aesthetic here — it's the contract that the tape and the replayer depend on. Everything in the effect model exists to preserve it.

What's here

  • Quickstart — write a handler, publish it, watch it serve.
  • Handlers — the activation kinds, the request/response surface, and the five verb roles.
  • Effects & replay — the determinism invariant, the four primitives, and durable vs ephemeral work.
Pre-launch. rewind.js is being built in the open and there's no public sign-up yet. These docs describe the handler contract as it stands today; runnable, edit-in-the-browser examples are on the way.
